key_insight: A thermodynamic, multiscale treatment shows that hydrogen segregation around a screw dislocation in bcc iron is governed by a few statistically relevant trapping configurations, especially the easy-core state.
engineering_relevance: This provides a physically grounded way to model hydrogen-dislocation interactions for predicting hydrogen embrittlement and solubility limits in iron-based materials.
method: Used molecular simulations with a neural-network interatomic potential to scan dislocation core states, site types, and hydrogen concentrations.
Built a thermodynamic framework to rank trapping configurations by statistical relevance.
Assessed when an elastic dipole approximation is valid as a simplified interaction model.
evidence_signal: The simulation-based energetics were compared against the limited density functional theory data available in the literature and showed good agreement.
watch_item: The simplified elastic dipole description is only valid within a restricted domain, so applying it outside that range may miss important core-specific effects.
paper_title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron
